g.data-narration: Welcome to the Evolution of Programming Languages - a journey from binary code to modern abstractions.
g.text: From Machine Code to Modern Multi-Paradigm Languages

g.data-narration: Understanding language evolution explains why compilers became necessary and increasingly sophisticated over time.
g.text: Each generation introduced new abstractions demanding

g.data-narration: The progression shows increasing abstraction from binary machine code to declarative domain-specific languages.
First-generation machine language uses binary instructions (0s and 1s) directly executed by the CPU hardware.
g.text: 1940s - Direct binary instructions executed by CPU

g.data-narration: Assembly language uses symbolic mnemonics like ADD and MOV, with one-to-one mapping to machine code via an assembler.
g.text: Late 1940s-1950s - Symbolic mnemonics translated by Assembler

g.data-narration: The key difference: assembly has one-to-one mapping while high-level languages have one-to-many mapping, increasing compiler complexity.
g.text: Assembler vs Compiler: One-to-One vs One-to-Many

g.data-narration: Third-generation languages like FORTRAN, COBOL, and ALGOL introduced variables, loops, and portability across machines.
g.text: 1950s-1960s - FORTRAN, COBOL, ALGOL - Variables, loops, conditionals

g.data-narration: C and Pascal introduced structured programming with control structures, strong typing, and efficient compiled code.
g.text: C (1972), Pascal (1970) - Enforced structured programming, better type systems

g.data-narration: Fourth-generation languages like SQL are declarative - specifying what you want, not how to get it.
Object-oriented programming organizes code around objects with data and methods, using encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism.
g.text: 1960s-Present - Simula, Smalltalk, C++, Java - Objects with data and methods

g.data-narration: Functional programming uses pure functions and immutability, avoiding changing state - languages include Lisp, Haskell, and ML.
g.text: 1950s-Present - Lisp, Haskell, ML - Pure functions, immutability, first-class functions

g.data-narration: Scripting languages emphasize rapid development with dynamic typing and interpreter-based execution for quick prototyping.
g.text: 1980s-Present - Python, JavaScript, Ruby - Dynamic typing, interpreted, rapid development

g.data-narration: Compare programming paradigms: procedural, object-oriented, functional, logical, and scripting with key strengths.
Higher-level language features increased compiler complexity, requiring phases like lexical analysis, parsing, semantic analysis, and optimization.
g.text: Higher abstractions required more sophisticated compilation phases

g.data-narration: Key takeaway: Understand why each generation emerged, not just chronology. Link language features to compiler requirements.
g.text: ✓ Focus on WHY each generation emerged, not just chronology
✓ Link language features to compiler requirements
✓ OOP features complicate compilation with inheritance
Module 1 connects syntax to translation via syntax-directed translation
